meta.note: South Africa apartheid-era pilot batch (Stratum A literary canon), Tier 1 high + Tier 2 medium. Deduped against the existing 37 ZA records and the wider DB. Kennis van die Aand (= Looking on Darkness, already in DB) and The Late Bourgeois World (already in DB) excluded as duplicates. Tier 3 author-level 'banned person' cases (Abrahams, Brutus) and the Fugard play held. The Autobiography of Malcolm X attaches to existing book id 67. Some Wikipedia-listed foreign titles lack a Gazette date; ban_year is a best estimate, noted in the description. Primary sources: McDonald 'The Literature Police' / theliteraturepolice.com, Government Gazette via Daily Maverick, SAHO, The Conversation.

entries.title: Down Second Avenue
entries.description_ban: Autobiography of growing up in apartheid Pretoria; banned after Mphahlele was listed under the Internal Security Act. A scanned 1967 censor's report on the book survives in McDonald's Literature Police archive. Permitted again after his December 1978 delisting.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban with a surviving censor's report; documented by The Literature Police (McDonald).
entries.source_name: The Literature Police (Peter D. McDonald) — 1967 censor's report

entries.title: Chocolates for My Wife
entries.description_ban: Autobiography by the Drum-generation writer and composer; banned by South African censors on publication in 1961, and among the titles unbanned in 1982.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Clear title-specific ban-and-unban; documented by South African History Online.

entries.title: Cry Rage!
entries.description_ban: Widely cited as the first poetry collection banned in apartheid South Africa, banned shortly after release for its militant Black Consciousness content.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Landmark first banned poetry collection; documented via Wikipedia with citation.

entries.title: Jol'iinkomo
entries.description_ban: Gwala's debut Black Consciousness poetry collection; banned by the Director of Publications, Cape Town, the same year it was published.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented by South African History Online.

entries.title: Call Me Not a Man
entries.description_ban: Staffrider-series short-story collection (Ravan Press) on Black life under apartheid; banned shortly after publication in November 1979.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; listed in McDonald's Literature Police webnotes.

entries.title: Seeds of War
entries.description_ban: Matshoba's second collection (Ravan, Staffrider series #11); banned in 1981.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; listed in McDonald's Literature Police webnotes.

entries.title: A Ride on the Whirlwind
entries.description_ban: Novel (Ad. Donker) about the Soweto uprising; banned on publication for its 'factual account' and 'idolising of black insurgents', then unbanned on the publisher's appeal.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban-and-appeal; documented in academic commentary (Africa in Words).

entries.title: Magersfontein, O Magersfontein!
entries.description_ban: CNA Award-winning Afrikaans satire banned by the Publications Appeal Board in November 1977 after a church-led campaign, despite the board conceding its high literary value. Unbanned 12 March 1980.
entries.inclusion_rationale: High-profile Afrikaans literary ban; reported by The Washington Post.

entries.title: Skryt
entries.description_ban: Afrikaans poetry collection (Skryt: Om 'n sinkende skip blou te verf); banned in 1975 over the poem 'Brief uit die vreemde aan slagter' addressed to PM Vorster, listing detainees who died in detention. The second Afrikaans literary work ever banned.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific Afrikaans ban; documented by Daily Maverick.

entries.title: In the Fog of the Seasons' End
entries.description_ban: Novel depicting the torture and death in detention of a resistance fighter; banned after censors objected to 'wild writing against the police' and held it threatened state security.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban with quoted censor verdict; documented by Archie Dick in The Conversation.

entries.title: Store Up the Anger
entries.description_ban: Fictionalised treatment of the Steve Biko detention-death case; banned before the local Ravan edition was even printed (the censors banned the cover and its replacement too).
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific pre-publication ban; documented in the author's account.

entries.title: Muriel at Metropolitan
entries.description_ban: The first English-language novel by a Black South African woman (Ravan Press, 1975, with extracts pre-cut); declared undesirable and banned in 1979. Circulated abroad as 'Between Two Worlds'.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Landmark title-specific ban; documented via Wikipedia with citation.

entries.title: The African Image
entries.description_ban: Seminal critical work; banned in South Africa after Mphahlele was listed under the Internal Security Act, until his 1978 delisting.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban within Mphahlele's listed-author suppression (medium confidence, Wikipedia).

entries.title: In Corner B and Other Stories
entries.description_ban: Short-story collection; banned under the Internal Security Act until Mphahlele's 1978 delisting.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban within Mphahlele's listed-author suppression (medium confidence, Wikipedia).

entries.title: The Wanderers
entries.description_ban: Mphahlele's first novel, on exile; banned in South Africa, reportedly remaining on the banned list even after his name was delisted in December 1978.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban (medium confidence, Wikipedia/Britannica).

entries.title: Voices in the Whirlwind and Other Essays
entries.description_ban: Essay collection; banned under the Internal Security Act, then among the titles permitted after the December 1978 delisting.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban (medium confidence, Wikipedia).

entries.title: The Will to Die
entries.description_ban: Posthumous short-story collection. Themba was declared a 'statutory communist' in 1966 and his works banned wholesale; could not be published or quoted in South Africa until the 1980s.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Banned within Themba's wholesale works-ban (medium confidence, author-level mechanism, Wikipedia).

entries.title: Home and Exile
entries.description_ban: Essay collection; banned under apartheid, with the banning specifically reviewed by the censors in 1976.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; documented in a peer-reviewed PEN history (medium confidence, abstract accessible).

entries.title: The Children of Soweto
entries.description_ban: Novel-trilogy on the 1976 Soweto uprising (Ravan, Staffrider series; later Longman edition 1982); listed as banned in McDonald's webnotes.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban; listed in McDonald's Literature Police webnotes (medium confidence).

entries.title: Occasion for Loving
entries.description_ban: Novel centred on a love affair between a white woman and a Black artist; banned shortly after publication for depicting an interracial relationship outlawed under apartheid. Exact Gazette date not located.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban asserted in academic literature (medium confidence; no Gazette date).
entries.source_name: Cal State scholarworks (thesis on apartheid literary censorship)

entries.title: A Walk in the Night
entries.description_ban: La Guma's debut novella; became prohibited reading inside South Africa on publication and was swept up in the blanket ban on all his writings as a 'listed person' from 1962.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific prohibition via listed-author ban (medium confidence; Britannica/Encyclopedia.com).

entries.title: And a Threefold Cord
entries.description_ban: Novel of Cape Flats shanty life; banned in South Africa during La Guma's lifetime under the blanket ban on his works as a listed person. Exact Gazette date not located; ban year set to publication year.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific prohibition via listed-author ban (medium confidence; Britannica).

entries.title: The Dawn Comes Twice
entries.description_ban: Cope novel of clandestine anti-apartheid resistance; belatedly banned in the late 1970s. Exact date not located; 1978 is an estimate.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific belated ban (medium confidence; Britannica).

entries.title: Elegy for a Revolutionary
entries.description_ban: Novel drawn from Driver's own underground political activity; banned in South Africa and only re-issued after 1994. Exact Gazette date not located.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Title-specific ban attested by publisher and obituary (medium confidence).

entries.title: The First Book of Africa
entries.description_ban: Children's non-fiction banned in apartheid South Africa for its celebration of Black African culture. Exact ban year unknown; set to publication year.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Listed in Wikipedia's South Africa banned-books section (medium confidence; no Gazette date).
entries.source_name: Wikipedia (List of books banned by governments)

entries.title: Soul on Ice
entries.description_ban: Banned in apartheid South Africa for its criticism of white supremacy and its sexual content. Exact ban year unknown; set to publication year.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Listed in Wikipedia's South Africa banned-books section (medium confidence; no Gazette date).
entries.source_name: Wikipedia (List of books banned by governments)

entries.title: Black Power: The Politics of Liberation
entries.description_ban: Foundational Black Power text banned in apartheid South Africa. Exact ban year unknown; set to publication year.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Listed in Wikipedia's South Africa banned-books section (medium confidence; no Gazette date).
entries.source_name: Wikipedia (List of books banned by governments)

entries.title: The Autobiography of Malcolm X
entries.description_ban: Banned in apartheid South Africa for its criticism of white supremacy. Exact ban year unknown; estimated. Adds a ZA ban to the existing book record.
entries.inclusion_rationale: Adds a South Africa ban to an existing book; listed in Wikipedia's SA banned-books section (medium confidence).
entries.source_name: Wikipedia (List of books banned by governments)
